journal-title-group.journal-title: Journal of magnetic resonance imaging : JMRI

title-group.article-title: Development and Deployment of a Machine Learning Model to Triage the Use of Prostate MRI (ProMT-ML) in Patients With Suspected Prostate Cancer

article-meta.aff: 1Department of Urology, NYU Grossman School of Medicine, New York, New York, USA
2Department of Radiology, NYU Grossman School of Medicine, New York, New York, USA
3Courant Institute of Mathematical Sciences, New York University, New York, New York, USA
4Department of Population Health, NYU Grossman School of Medicine, New York, New York, USA
5Department of Urology, Bellevue Hospital, New York City Health and Hospitals, New York, New York, USA

license.license-p: This file is available for text mining. It may also be used consistent with the principles of fair use under the copyright law.

sec.title: Background:
sec.p: Access to prostate MRI remains limited due to resource constraints and the need for expert interpretation.

sec.title: Purpose:
sec.p: To develop machine learning (ML) models that enable risk-based triage for prostate MRI (ProMT-ML) in the evaluation of prostate cancer.

sec.title: Study Type:
sec.p: Retrospective and prospective.

sec.title: Population:
sec.p: A total of 11,879 retrospective MRI scans for suspected prostate cancer from a multi-hospital health system, divided into training (N = 9504) and test (N = 2375) sets. A total of 4551 records for prospective validation.

sec.title: Field Strength/Sequence:
sec.p: 1.5T and 3T/Turbo-spin echo T2-weighted imaging (T2WI), diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI), and dynamic contrast-enhanced (DCE).

sec.title: Assessment:
sec.p: Prostate Imaging Reporting and Data System (PI-RADS) scores were retrieved from MRI reports. The Boruta algorithm was used to select final input features from candidate features. Two models were developed using supervised ML to estimate the likelihood of an abnormal MRI, defined as PI-RADS ≥ 3: Model A (with prostate volume) and Model B (without prostate volume). Models were compared to PSA. Prostate biopsy pathology was assessed to evaluate potential clinical impact.

sec.title: Statistical Tests:
sec.p: Area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC) was the primary performance metric.

sec.title: Results:
sec.p: A total of 5580 (46.9%) subjects had a PI-RADS score ≥ 3. After feature selection, Model A included age, PSA, body mass index, and prostate volume, while Model B included age, PSA, body mass index, and systolic blood pressure. Both models A (AUC 0.711) and B (AUC 0.616) significantly outperformed PSA (AUC 0.593). Compared to PSA threshold > 4 ng/mL, Model A demonstrated significantly improved specificity (28.3% vs. 21.9%) and no significant difference in sensitivity (89.0% vs. 86.7%). Among false negatives (Model A: 8.0% (62/776); Model B: 16.8% (130/776)), most (Model A: 87%; Model B: 69%) had benign or clinically insignificant disease on biopsy. On prospective validation, both versions of ProMT-ML significantly outperformed PSA.

sec.title: Data Conclusion:
sec.p: ProMT-ML provides personalized risk estimates of abnormal prostate MRI and can support triage of this test.

sec.title: Technical Efficacy:
sec.p: Stage 4.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Prostate magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is an important diagnostic tool for prostate cancer (PCa): it identifies suspicious regions in the prostate to target for subsequent biopsy [1] and increases the detection of clinically significant PCa [2-4]. Additionally, negative prostate MRI may obviate the need for biopsy and reduce the overdiagnosis of clinically insignificant PCa [1, 3, 5]. The diagnostic value of the technique has led to an increase in its utilization over time [6-10].

Although guidelines recommend prostate MRI prior to biopsy, use of prostate MRI before biopsy remains highly variable [11-13]. Disparities in access disproportionately affect racial and ethnic minorities, as well as patients from lower-income and underinsured backgrounds, exacerbating inequities in PCa care [10, 14-16]. Contributing factors include financial costs, inconsistent insurance coverage, limited availability of 3T MRI scanners which is the preferred field strength, and a shortage of radiologists with specialized expertise for accurate prostate MRI interpretation [17, 18]. Additionally, growing demand for prostate MRI can lead to extended wait times, potentially delaying diagnosis and treatment. Moreover, the potential environmental impact of prostate MRI utilization based on the estimated greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions associated with this test underscores the importance of optimizing its use [19]. Given that PCa is the most frequently diagnosed malignancy in the United States [20] and one of the most common cancers worldwide [21], optimizing the use of prostate MRI is an important task.

While clinical guidelines endorse prostate MRI before initial biopsy, they provide limited guidance on selecting patients for imaging [11-13]. A risk-based triage tool could optimize prostate MRI use, facilitating timely diagnosis while potentially reducing unnecessary imaging in low–risk patients.

Thus the aim of this study was to develop a machine learning (ML)-based classification model (ProMT-ML) that estimates the likelihood of an abnormal prostate MRI based on clinical features that are routinely available in the electronic health record (EHR).

sec.title: Data Source and Patient Selection
sec.p: The study was approved by the NYU Langone Health institutional review board, along with a waiver of written informed consent for the use of de-identified clinical information.

We used the NYU Urology Datahub, a repository of EHR data, to identify patients who underwent a prostate MRI between October 2011 and June 2024. Our institution is an academic health system comprised of three major hospitals and numerous additional smaller clinical sites, serving a diverse metropolitan population of approximately 20.1 million residents. The Datahub contains comprehensive longitudinal data, including demographics, diagnoses, procedures, clinical notes, and laboratory results. These data have been previously used to develop ML models [22].

Patients were excluded if: (i) they had a prior PCa diagnosis, (ii) the prostate MRI report did not include an assigned Prostate Imaging Reporting and Data System (PI-RADS) score [23], and (iii) a prostate-specific antigen (PSA) measurement within 6 months before MRI was unavailable.

sec.title: Patient Sample
sec.p: We identified 65,960 prostate MRI records from 2011 to 2024. After applying exclusion criteria (Figure S1), the final cohort consisted of 11,879 patients (Table 1). We note that of those without a diagnosis of PCa and available PSA and PI-RADS score, 0.42% (51/12,263) did not have a prostate volume available.

In the overall sample, median age was 65 (IQR: 60–71) years, median PSA was 5.8 (IQR: 4.4–8.4) ng/mL, and 5580 (46.9%) were found to have an abnormal prostate MRI (PI-RADS ≥ 3) (Table 1). The dataset was randomly split into a training set (80%, n = 9504) and a test set (20%, n = 2375), with their characteristics detailed in Table 1.

To further assess the validity of our model on an unseen sample, we identified a prospective sample of 4551 patients undergoing a prostate MRI between July 2024 and September 2025. In this sample, the median age was 66 (IQR: 61–72) years, the median PSA was 5.5 (IQR: 3.7–9.5) ng/mL, and 1804 (39.6%) were found to have an abnormal prostate MRI (Table 1).

sec.title: Model Input Features
sec.p: As candidate input features for the classification models, age, race/ethnicity, body mass index, diastolic blood pressure, systolic blood pressure, history of diabetes mellitus, history of hypertension, history of tobacco use, PSA, prostate volume, use of 5-alpha reductase inhibitor medication, history of a prior prostate MRI, and history of a prior abnormal prostate MRI were extracted from the EHRs.

Race and ethnicity features in the EHR were used to categorize patients into the National Institute of Health race subgroups [24]. Prostate volume was obtained from the prostate MRI radiology report. Our motivation for including prostate volume in the model was based on the well-established relationship between PSA density, derived from PSA and prostate volume, and PCa [25]. Furthermore, this data can be available relatively easily in the clinical setting in patients without a previous prostate MRI if they have had previous cross-sectional or transrectal ultrasound imaging, a situation we have encountered frequently in our health system. We used MRI-derived prostate volume as a practical surrogate for patients with previous cross-sectional or transrectal imaging as volume measurements have shown to be similar across modalities [26] and were easily available in the EHR data as it is a standard reporting item in the MRI report. To account for the clinical situation where prostate volume is unavailable, two complementary models were developed: Model A (including prostate volume) and Model B (excluding prostate volume). Prostate volume was calculated using the traditional prostate ellipsoid formula (length × width × height × 0.52) [27]. Raw PSA values that were not adjusted for the use of 5-alpha reductase inhibitor were used in subsequent analyses.

sec.title: Outcome
sec.p: Prostate MRI is reported on a standardized scale known as the PI-RADS score. This score indicates the likelihood of malignancy and ranges in value from 1 to 5 [23]. In a triage-focused PCa diagnostic pathway, patients with an abnormal MRI (PI-RADS score ≥ 3) typically undergo targeted and systematic biopsy, while those with normal MRI (PI-RADS score 1 to 2) may avoid biopsy [28]. To align with this strategy, the outcome of interest for our classification models was binary and defined as the presence of an abnormal prostate MRI (PI-RAD ≥ 3). This supports a triage-focused approach, prioritizing patients at higher estimated risk for abnormal prostate MRI while enabling lower-risk individuals to be evaluated in a less-urgent manner or reassessed for MRI necessity. Given the range of years included in the study, reports based on both PI-RADS version 1 and PI-RADS version 2.1 are included.

sec.title: Feature Selection
sec.p: To optimize ease of clinical application, support model interpretability, and mitigate potential overfitting of the classification model, feature selection on the candidate input features was performed to reduce the final set of input features and create a parsimonious model.

The Boruta algorithm was used to perform feature selection. This algorithm uses a random forest classifier with permuted features to assess the importance of the actual features to identify and remove highly correlated features. This method has been widely used as a feature selection method to develop ML models [29, 30].

sec.title: Model Development
sec.p: Age, body mass index, PSA, systolic blood pressure, diastolic blood pressure, and prostate volume were continuous features; history of hypertension, diabetes, use of 5-alpha reductase inhibitor, prior prostate MRI, and prior abnormal prostate MRI were binary features, and race was a categorical feature. Continuous features were scaled while categorical variables were one-hot encoded.

In the training sample, 13 ML classification models (Logistic Regression, Gradient Boosting, AdaBoost, CatBoost, Decision Tree, Random Forest, Extra Trees, LightGBM, XGBoost, Quadratic Discriminant Analysis, Naïve Bayes, Support Vector Machine, and Balanced Random Forest), representing a set of both ensemble and non-ensemble ML algorithms, were compared [31]. Grid-search with five-fold cross-validation was used to tune hyperparameters (Table S1) and the models were optimized for area under the curve (AUC). Using the selected hyperparameters, the models were then trained in the full training sample and model performance was assessed in the test sample.

To align with a triage-focused diagnostic pathway, high sensitivity (≥ 80%) was prioritized to minimize the risk of missing clinically significant cancers while accepting a compromise on specificity. Model performance for estimating PI-RADS ≥ 3 was determined and compared with that of a traditional PSA-based approach.

sec.title: Model Evaluation
sec.p: The primary performance metric for model evaluation was area under the receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve (AUC) and the secondary performance metric was F1 score.

To compare the developed models with the PSA-based approach, ROC curves were plotted to compare the range of estimated probabilities of our models against PSA as a continuous value. Additional comparisons included sensitivity, specificity, positive predictive value (PPV), and negative predictive value (NPV) using the pre-defined threshold of our models against traditional PSA thresholds of > 3 and > 4 ng/mL [11, 12].

We note that identical training and test populations were used for both Model A and Model B to allow for direct comparison of model performance. The difference between the models was in training, where Model A was exposed to prostate volume as a candidate feature while Model B was not.

To assess the potential clinical implications of false-negative misclassifications, that is, potentially delayed diagnosis due to the triage model falsely estimating a normal prostate MRI, biopsy pathology results were reviewed in patients who underwent biopsy within 180 days of prostate MRI.

sec.title: Statistical Analysis
sec.p: We estimated 95% confidence intervals (CIs) around the AUCs using 1000 bootstrap samples. AUCs were compared with non-parametric bootstrap tests with two-sided p values. We estimated 95% CIs around sensitivity and specificity using the Wilson score method. We compared sensitivity and specificity of the models against the PSA thresholds using McNemar's test. We used the χ2 test with pairwise post hoc comparisons with Bonferroni correction to compare the proportions of clinically significant disease in patients with false-negative classifications that subsequently underwent prostate biopsy.
A p value < 0.05 was considered statistically significant. All analyses were completed using Python 3.8.3 with packages from Scikit-learn and BorutaShap [32].

sec.title: Feature Selection
sec.p: When all demographic, clinical, and disease-specific candidate features were considered as inputs to the model, the Boruta algorithm selected a final set of four input features: age, PSA, body mass index, and prostate volume. When applying the algorithm after excluding prostate volume as a candidate feature, four input features were selected: age, PSA, body mass index, and systolic blood pressure.

sec.title: Model Development and Evaluation
sec.p: Using the selected features, the 13 supervised ML classification models were trained on the training set (n = 9504). Hyperparameter tuning details are provided in Table S1.

For Model A, the top three models in the training set based on AUC were Balanced Random Forest (AUC: 0.863; 95% CI: 0.855–0.870), followed by Random Forest (AUC: 0.836; 95% CI: 0.828–0.844), and Extra Trees (AUC: 0.834; 95% CI: 0.827–0.841) (Figure 1A and Table S2). In the test sample, the top three models were CatBoost (AUC: 0.711; 95% CI: 0.689–0.731), followed by Gradient Boosting (AUC: 0.708; 95% CI: 0.688–0.728) and AdaBoost (AUC: 0.707; 95% CI: 0.686–0.727) (Figure 1A).

For Model B, the top three models in the training set based on AUC were Balanced Random Forest (AUC: 0.798; 95% CI: 0.789–0.807), followed by Random Forest (AUC: 0.778; 95% CI: 0.769–0.787), and Extra Trees (AUC: 0.653; 95% CI: 0.643–0.663) (Figure 1B). In the test sample, the top three models were CatBoost (AUC: 0.616; 95% CI: 0.593–0.638), and Gradient Boosting (AUC: 0.616; 95% CI: 0.594–0.640) and AdaBoost (AUC: 0.616; 95% CI: 0.595–0.637) (Figure 1B).
The AUC and F1 scores for all training and testing evaluations are shown in Table S2 for Model A and Table S3 for Model B.

Based on these results, CatBoost was selected as the best algorithm for both Model A and Model B, as it achieved the highest AUC on the test set for Model A and outperformed other algorithms with similar AUC scores in terms of F1 score for Model B.

sec.title: Threshold Determination
sec.p: For Model A, we selected a threshold of 30% which yielded a sensitivity of 89.0%, specificity of 28.3%, PPV of 50.8% and NPV of 75.5% (Table S4). For Model B, we selected a threshold of 40% which yielded a sensitivity of 80.5%, specificity of 30.5%, PPV of 49.1% and NPV of 65.3% (Table S5).

sec.title: Comparing Models Against PSA
sec.p: ROC curves comparing Model A, Model B, and PSA are shown in Figure 2. Model A had a significantly higher AUC than Model B (AUC difference: 0.095, 95% CI: 0.075–0.115) and PSA (AUC: difference 0.117, 95% CI: 0.093–0.141). Model B also had a significantly higher AUC than PSA (AUC difference: 0.022, 95% CI: 0.003–0.041).

Table 2 compares sensitivity, specificity, PPV, and NPV of Models A and B at their pre-specified thresholds against PSA thresholds of > 3 and > 4 ng/mL. A PSA threshold of > 3 ng/mL had the highest sensitivity (94.3%) but the lowest specificity (13.4%). Model A had high sensitivity (89.0%) and higher specificity (28.3%). Model B had the highest specificity (30.5%) but lower sensitivity (80.5%) than Model A.

sec.title: Clinical Audit
sec.p: Among the 776 patients who underwent biopsy within 180 days of MRI, cases where the models and PSA-threshold approaches incorrectly estimated a normal MRI (i.e., false negatives) were audited (Model A: 62/776 (8.0%); Model B: 130/776 (16.8%); PSA > 3: 31/776 (4.0%); PSA > 4: 79/776 (10.2%)), and compared with biopsy findings (Figure 3). Model A had the highest proportion of false negatives with benign pathology or clinically insignificant disease (87.1%), indicating that most misclassified patients did not have concerning underlying disease. PSA > 4 had a significantly higher proportion (36.7% (29/79)) of patients with high-grade disease (grade group 4 or 5) misclassified as false negatives compared to Model A (12.9% (8/62)).

sec.title: Prospective Evaluation
sec.p: In the prospective sample, both versions of ProMT-ML (Model A—AUC: 0.642; 95% CI: 0.625–0.659; Model B—AUC: 0.575; 95% CI: 0.558–0.592) significantly outperformed PSA (AUC difference Model A vs. PSA: 0.083 (95% CI: 0.070–0.095); Model B vs. PSA: 0.015 (95% CI: 0.006–0.025)). In this sample, Model A had the highest sensitivity (82.9%) and Model B had the highest specificity (34.7%) (Table 3).

sec.title: Model Deployment
sec.p: ProMT-ML was deployed as an application at https://nayanlab.shinyapps.io/promt_ml/ which can be accessed through a web or mobile browser. Through this application, the user inputs four features and probability estimates are generated for the likelihood of an abnormal prostate MRI.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: Despite its clinical importance, a standardized tool to optimize prostate MRI use based on individual patient risk is lacking. To address this gap, ProMT-ML, a ML-based classification model utilizing routinely available clinical data, was developed to quantify the likelihood of an abnormal prostate MRI (PI-RADS score ≥ 3). Our models achieved high sensitivity and offered significantly higher specificity than traditional PSA-based thresholds. These models retained strong performance on prospective validation and continued to outperform PSA, further strengthening their validity. By prioritizing high-risk patients for imaging, ProMT-ML has the potential to facilitate timely diagnosis, reduce unnecessary imaging, and alleviate strain on healthcare resources.

Recognizing real-world variability in the availability of prostate volume data, two versions of ProMT-ML were developed. The model incorporating prostate volume demonstrated significantly superior performance, reinforcing the well-established relationship between PSA, prostate volume, and PCa risk [33]. This finding highlights the importance of prostate volume as a key feature in PCa risk models. While our study relied on MRI-reported prostate volume, volume estimates may also be reliably obtained from prior pelvic cross-sectional imaging or ultrasound [26], and less reliably based on digital rectal exam [34]. When prostate volume was unavailable, systolic blood pressure was selected as a feature. While systolic blood pressure is not traditionally associated with prostate MRI outcomes, a meta-analysis of 21 studies found that a diagnosis of hypertension was associated with an 8% increased risk of PCa [35]. Additionally, a previous population-based cohort study reported that systolic blood pressure > 150 mmHg was associated with a 35% increased risk of PCa, and both systolic and diastolic blood pressure correlated with PCa mortality [36]. A potential mechanistic link between hypertension and PCa may involve androgen-mediated activity, which plays a role in both conditions [37, 38]. A previous study has also investigated the link between anti-hypertensive medications and PCa risk, but further studies are needed [39]. While systolic blood pressure may not add substantial value when prostate volume is available, its inclusion in ProMT-ML when prostate volume is missing is biologically plausible and warrants further investigation as a risk factor for PCa.

A key finding of our study is that ProMT-ML improves patient selection for prostate MRI compared to traditional PSA-based approaches. Current National Comprehensive Cancer Network guidelines recommend prostate MRI for patients with a PSA > 3 ng/mL [12]. Our study confirms that this threshold lacks specificity, leading to excessive imaging and increased healthcare burden. In contrast, ProMT-ML maintains high sensitivity while substantially improving specificity, potentially allowing a more refined triage strategy. As a clinical decision support tool, ProMT-ML has the potential to be used to prioritize prostate MRI in high-risk patients, facilitating timely diagnosis and intervention. For lower-risk individuals, health systems may consider a non-urgent queue, while those with minimal risk could be reassessed for MRI necessity based on their overall clinical profile.

Beyond performance metrics, we sought to assess the clinical implications of ProMT-ML as a triage-focused strategy by evaluating biopsy outcomes in patients misclassified as having a normal MRI (false negatives). Our findings indicate that the majority of these patients had benign or clinically insignificant disease. Notably, when using Model A (which includes prostate volume), no false-negative cases were found to have high-grade disease, whereas Model B (without prostate volume) performed comparably to PSA-based approaches. These results suggest that ProMT-ML may provide a more individualized and precise risk assessment than a fixed PSA threshold while maintaining clinical safety in terms of false-negative classification.

ML and artificial intelligence are increasingly being incorporated into PCa classification models [40-43]. One such model, ClarityDX, was recently developed to aid in patient selection for prostate biopsy [40]. Based on five clinical and disease-specific features, ClarityDX demonstrated high sensitivity (96%) and moderate specificity (32%) in detecting clinically significant disease. The authors suggested using ClarityDX as a reflex test for men with elevated PSA to determine whether MRI is necessary. Similarly, ProMT-ML could serve as a reflex test for MRI selection, potentially optimizing resource allocation and improving clinical decision-making for men with elevated PSA.

Our study has several key strengths. First, we developed ProMT-ML using a multi-institutional dataset, ensuring broad applicability across different populations, including racial and socioeconomic minorities. Second, ProMT-ML relies on routinely collected clinical parameters, making it practical for wide-spread implementation. Third, we demonstrated that clinically significant cancers were rarely missed, particularly with Model A (prostate volume included). Fourth, we deployed ProMT-ML as an interactive web-based tool (https://nayanlab.shinyapps.io/promt_ml/), allowing clinicians to easily input patient data and obtain real-time risk estimates. Fifth, our model is unique in estimating the likelihood of an abnormal prostate MRI, compared to existing models for PCa diagnosis or prognosis [40, 44, 45]. Sixth, we prospectively validated our models to enhance generalizability and clinical applicability.

sec.title: Limitations
sec.p: While ProMT-ML performed well in our dataset, external validation is needed across diverse populations and healthcare settings to confirm its generalizability. This step is critical before a model can be deployed at scale. Furthermore, our model was constrained by the structured data available within the EHRs. Future versions of the model may be improved by incorporating additional clinical parameters, such as digital rectal exam findings [40]. Additionally, given the prolonged timeframe of this study, there were different versions of the PI-RADS scoring system that were used. Finally, while ProMT-ML improves risk stratification, its real-world effect on resource utilization, cost-effectiveness, and clinical outcomes remains to be evaluated in prospective evaluation.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: We developed and deployed ProMT-ML, a ML-based model that provides individualized risk estimates for an abnormal prostate MRI (PI-RADS score ≥ 3). By supporting a triage-focused approach in PCa diagnosis, ProMT-ML has the potential to improve timely detection of clinically significant disease, optimize resource allocation, and reduce unnecessary imaging.

sec.title: Supplementary Material
sec.p: Additional supporting information can be found online in the Supporting Information section. Data S1: jmri70162-sup-0001-Supinfo.pdf.

caption.p: Comparing area under the curve across machine learning models estimating likelihood of abnormal prostate MRI, with (A) and without (B) prostate volume as a predictor.
Comparison of ROC curves for Model A, Model B, and prostate-specific antigen (PSA).
Audit of prostate biopsy pathology findings in model predicted false negatives and PSA threshold approaches.
Characteristics of patients included for the development and evaluation of machine learning-based classification models to estimate the likelihood of having an abnormal prostate MRI (PI-RADS ≥ 3).

tr.td: History of prior abnormal prostate MRI (n (%))

caption.p: Comparing models against PSA thresholds to estimate the risk of an abnormal prostate MRI (PI-RADS score ≥ 3) in the retrospective testing sample.

fn.p: Note: Statistically significant differences (p < 0.05) are indicated by, *Model vs. PSA > 3 ng/mL and **Model vs. PSA > 4 ng/mL.

fn.label: a
fn.p: Model A features: age, PSA, BMI, and prostate volume.

fn.label: b
fn.p: Model B features: age, PSA, BMI, and systolic blood pressure.

caption.p: Comparing models against PSA thresholds to estimate risk of an abnormal prostate MRI (PI-RADS score ≥ 3) in a prospective validation sample.

fn.p: Note: Statistically significant differences (p < 0.05) are indicated by, *Model vs. PSA > 3 ng/mL and **Model vs. PSA > 4 ng/mL.

fn.label: a
fn.p: Model A features: age, PSA, BMI, and prostate volume.

fn.label: b
fn.p: Model B features: age, PSA, BMI, and systolic blood pressure.

boxed-text.p: Prostate MRI is increasingly used in the evaluation of prostate cancer but is not widely available. To optimize its use, we developed ProMT-ML, a machine learning model that uses four routinely available clinical features to provide personalized risk estimates of abnormal prostate MRI findings. The model demonstrated strong accuracy, is deployed online, and can support triage by prioritizing higher-risk patients for imaging while allowing lower-risk patients to be evaluated in a non-urgent setting.
